message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Australia is currently experiencing COVID-19 outbreaks from infection with SARS-CoV-2 Delta variants (B.1.617.2, AY.3). Analysis of the index case reveals a sub-consensus level of sequencing reads (∼25%) that support a 17-nucleotide deletion in ORF7a (ORF7a
                  <jats:sup>Δ17del</jats:sup>
                  ). ORF7a
                  <jats:sup>Δ17del</jats:sup>
                  induces a frameshift mutation in ORF7a, which truncates the peptide and potentially leads to reduced suppression of host restriction factor BST-2/CD317/Tetherin. Despite this, the mutation has rapidly become represented at the consensus level in subsequent cases: approximately 72% of SARS-CoV-2 genomes in the Australian outbreak possess ORF7a
                  <jats:sup>Δ17del</jats:sup>
                  , and 99.7% (1534/1538) of Delta genomes on GISAID with ORF7a
                  <jats:sup>Δ17del</jats:sup>
                  originate from the current Australian outbreak (5 August 2021). The global abundance of this mutation might be underestimated given the difficulty of variant calling software correctly calling insertion/deletions (indels), the common inability of phylogenetics software to take indels into account, and the tendency of GISAID to not release submissions that contain a frameshift mutation (unless specifically requested). Overall, the rapid increase of persistent ORF7a
                  <jats:sup>Δ17del</jats:sup>
                  variants is concerning, and suggests either a chance founder effect with a neutral mutation yet to be purged, or that the ORF7a
                  <jats:sup>Δ17del</jats:sup>
                  mutation provides a direct selective advantage.
                </jats:p>
title: Rapid spread of a SARS-CoV-2 Delta variant with a frameshift deletion in ORF7a

reference.article-title: An 81-Nucleotide Deletion in SARS-CoV-2 ORF7a Identified from Sentinel Surveillance in Arizona
Functional landscape of SARS-CoV-2 cellular restriction
BST2/CD317 counteracts human coronavirus 229E productive infection by tethering virions at the cell surface

reference.unstructured: Saito A , Irie T , Suzuki R , et al. SARS-CoV-2 spike P681R mutation, a hallmark of the Delta variant, enhances viral fusogenicity and pathogenicity. bioRxiv 2021;2021.06.17.448820.
